nativeRange.habitat: Ponds and forest creeks rich in plant debris; slow or still blackwater over soft substrate with heavy leaf litter and submerged wood

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 3–5 cm deep for burrowing
dried leaf litter (oak or beech leaves) for camouflage and refuge
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
South American biotope planting (Amazonian swords, java fern, anubias)

diet.notes: Nocturnal sit-and-wait micro-predator feeding on small invertebrates and organic matter along the substrate. Accepts live and frozen bloodworms, earthworms, tubifex, and brine shrimp; will take quality sinking pellets and wafers over time. Feed after lights out. Does not compete well at feeding time with faster tankmates — target feeding is often required.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (e.g., neon, ember, cardinal)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving species that outcompete at feeding
species requiring bright lighting or high water movement

summary: A cryptic, banjo-shaped Amazonian catfish that lies motionless on the substrate, camouflaged as a dead leaf. Strictly nocturnal; scaleless armor makes it copper-sensitive. Intermediate care due to specialist feeding and secretive nature.
careNotes:
The Banjo Catfish earns its name from a distinctly flat, banjo-shaped body — a broad, flattened head tapering to a slender tail — covered in tubercle-studded, scaleless skin that mimics leaf litter on the substrate. A purely nocturnal sit-and-wait micro-predator, it will remain motionless and all-but-invisible during daylight hours, emerging only after lights out to forage. A fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep is important for this species, which will partly bury itself; dried leaf litter (oak or beech) and bogwood are strongly recommended to provide daytime cover and enrich the biotope. Dim or diffuse lighting is preferred; intense lighting causes prolonged hiding and feeding refusal. Because the skin is scaleless and heavily textured, the Banjo Catfish is sensitive to copper-based medications and should never be treated with standard copper or organophosphate medications — use copper-free alternatives and dose at half-strength for any medication when in doubt. Soft, acidic to neutral water (pH 5.8–7.8, gH 2–20) replicates Amazonian blackwater conditions; a small amount of leaf litter or peat filtration is beneficial. Target-feed after lights out with live or frozen bloodworms, earthworms, or brine shrimp — the fish will not compete with daytime feeders and can starve in a busy community tank if feeding is not managed. Conspecifics are tolerated well; groups of three or more are recommended for more natural behavior. Care is rated intermediate due to nocturnal feeding demands, copper sensitivity, and the need for soft, low-light biotope conditions. NOTE: Dysichthys coracoideus is the former name applied to this species; Dysichthys was synonymized with Bunocephalus by Friel (1995) in the comprehensive revision of Aspredinidae. The current valid name is Bunocephalus coracoideus (Cope, 1874). Entry fw-fish-261 will be merged with fw-fish-262 (banjo-catfish-bunocephalus) at end-of-slice cleanup.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-262 (Banjo Catfish / Bunocephalus coracoideus). Dysichthys coracoideus synonymised into Bunocephalus coracoideus by Friel 1994/95 Aspredinidae revision. Current valid name retained.
breedingNotes: Rarely achieved in captivity. Requires a group of at least six mature specimens and a species or biotope tank with fine sandy substrate. Spawning occurs at night on the substrate; the female deposits 4,000–5,000 eggs onto sandy substrate, which hatch in approximately three days. Fry accept microworm or Artemia nauplii immediately after absorbing the yolk sac. No parental care is provided. Sexual dimorphism is subtle — ripe females appear more rounded in the abdomen.

No explicit mention of Dysichthys synonymy on Wikipedia page; Friel 1995 synonymy documented per primary task brief.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely achieved in captivity. Requires group of ≥6 mature specimens, fine sand substrate, and a quiet biotope tank. Female deposits 4,000–5,000 eggs on sandy substrate at night; eggs hatch in ~3 days. Fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii.
